title: Partitioned Iterative Quantum Scheduling of Satellites for Urgent Disaster Response: Case study of Wildfire
authors: Lucas T. Braydwood, Taejin Park, Hirofumi Hashimoto, Zoe Gonzalez Izquierdo, Andrew Michaelis, Eleanor Rieffel, Shon Grabbe
abstract: The standard in Earth-observation tasks today is having near real-time access to surface images in response to changing conditions. For instance, as urban environments interface more with wildlands and wildfires become less predictable, their tracking with satellite resources becomes essential. This requires the coordination of increasingly large constellations of satellites, giving rise to challenging computational problems. With wildfire detection and tracking as a backdrop, we investigate the power of special purpose and novel computing paradigms to tackle the ensuing satellite scheduling problems, making a compelling case for quantum algorithms. We bring quantum scheduling algorithms closer to implementation by examining both the emerging iterative quantum algorithm framework, which comes with analytic guarantees compared to some classical algorithms, and distributed quantum computing methods whose relevance is on the rise as utility-scale problems begin to get solved with quantum computers. Drawing strength from several computing fronts, we develop a distributed/parallelization scheme in conjunction with the quantum algorithm design and apply these techniques to real-world datasets for wildfire detection. While our quantum subprocesses are currently too small to see significant quantum advantage, our results validate the utility of these techniques, and continue forging the path toward distributed quantum computing.
summary: This paper tackles the computationally hard problem of scheduling satellites for wildfire monitoring by formulating it as a Maximum Independent Set problem. It proposes using advanced quantum computing paradigms, including iterative and distributed quantum algorithms, to solve this resource allocation challenge. While current simulations show classical methods leading, the methodology advances the theoretical path toward solving utility-scale problems on future quantum hardware.
main_problem: Developing efficient quantum algorithms to solve the NP-hard problem of scheduling satellite observations for near real-time wildfire tracking.
main_result: The work validates the utility of iterative and distributed quantum techniques for this resource allocation problem, despite current hardware limitations preventing a demonstrated quantum advantage over classical solvers.
method: The scheduling problem is mapped to finding the Maximum Independent Set (MIS) on a graph, which is then formulated as a QUBO/Ising model suitable for QAOA or iterative quantum algorithms.
model_or_system: The system involves coordinating large constellations of satellites observing wildfire targets derived from GOES-16 multi-spectral data. The computational model uses MIS formulation and explores partitioned/distributed quantum processing units (QPUs).
key_observables: Achieved Reward/Number of Requests; Expectation values ($\langle \sigma^z_i angle$); Fire size, Fire Radiative Power (FRP).
important_parameters: Lagrange multiplier ($\lambda=2$); Partition size ($m$, limited to $\sim 18$ qubits in simulations); Number of satellites/requests.
main_assumptions: The current quantum subprocesses are too small to show significant quantum advantage; the scheduling problem is modeled by mapping constraints onto an MIS graph structure.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate sample GOES-16 fire detection overlaid with WUI data, and compare simulation results of classical greedy vs. partitioned MIN/MINQ/MMQ across varying request numbers.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the real-world scheduling challenge (wildfire tracking) to formulating it as an MIS problem, then detailing quantum approaches like QAOA and iterative algorithms (MINQ/MMQ), culminating in a methodology for partitioning large problems into manageable sub-problems for distributed computation.
why_interesting: The focus on mapping complex combinatorial optimization problems onto structured quantum Hamiltonians ($\hat{C}$) and developing strategies (partitioning, iteration) to handle system sizes beyond current hardware capabilities is highly relevant to the theory of quantum algorithms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a) Sample GOES-16 FDC imagery from Aug. 18, 2020 at 00:01 UTC. Red denotes an active fire area, green regions with no fire, yellow clouds, and blue water. (b) Corre- sponding WUI image from the U.S. Forest Service [29]. Yellow and orange indicate wildland urban interface and intermix, re- spectively. No housing indicated in dark green and very low housing density shown in medium green. Low and very low housing density in light green, medium and high housing den- sity in maroon, and water indicated in blue.

FIG. 2: Lines are orbital tracks in one-second increments for CARTOSAT-2e (blue line), KAZEOSAT-1 (yellow line), and HYSIS (green line) on July 25, 2023. Orbital tracks have been synchronized to pass over the CA coast in unison. Red filled polygons are the intersection of GOES FireTracks data from July 19 – Sept 28, 2020 with the WUI low/med/high data. Rectangles are sample, notional field of views (FOVs).

FIG. 3: Simulation results for three different satellites, using the classical algorithms MIN and partitioned MIN as well as two quantum algorithms, both partitioned. The y axis represents the achieved reward of the algorithm divided by the number of requests, with a value of 1 corresponding to satisfying every request from an optimal angle. The data is averaged over all instances for the indicated satellites with higher request simulations coming from longer time bins.
